title: Eurozone GDP Outpaces Estimates with 0.3% Growth
content: Fresh data released by Eurostat reveals the Eurozone economy grew by 0.3% in the final quarter of 2025, defying consensus expectations of a sharper slowdown. The performance was bolstered by resilience in Germany and Spain, suggesting that the European Central Bank's previous rate cuts are beginning to transmit effectively into the real economy.
summary: Eurozone Q4 GDP growth of 0.3% beats forecasts, driven by major economies and easing financial conditions.
context:
• The European Central Bank (ECB) has delivered approximately 200 basis points of rate cuts since mid-2024 to combat stagnation.

• Manufacturing in the bloc, particularly in Germany, has faced prolonged headwinds from high energy costs and cooling global demand.

• Market positioning had been increasingly defensive as traders weighed the impact of potential US trade tariffs on European exports.
happened:
• Eurostat reported quarterly GDP growth of 0.3% for both the Euro area and the EU, surpassing the 0.2% expected by many analysts.

• Full-year 2025 growth for the Eurozone was confirmed at a solid 1.4%, supported by a rebound in household consumption.

• The Euro (EUR/USD) edged higher toward $1.0950 following the release as traders slightly pared back expectations for aggressive near-term ECB easing.
implications:
• Improved growth data may provide the ECB with room to pause its cutting cycle if core inflation remains sticky.

• Stronger-than-expected activity in Spain and Italy points toward a widening growth divergence within the bloc.

• Renewed optimism in European equities is likely if domestic demand continues to offset external trade uncertainties.
importance:
• Provides a critical update on the health of the world's second-largest economic bloc.

• Influences the terminal rate expectations for the ECB, a key driver for global bond markets.

• Serves as a barometer for whether the Eurozone can withstand escalating global trade tensions.

title: US Producer Prices Slow More Than Expected
content: The US Producer Price Index (PPI) rose by just 0.3% in February, coming in below market forecasts and signaling a cooling of inflationary pressures at the factory gate. This data has reinforced market hopes that the Federal Reserve may have sufficient evidence to consider interest rate cuts later this year.
summary: US PPI inflation eased to 3.2% annually in February, providing relief to markets worried about persistent price pressures.
content:
• The Federal Reserve has maintained a 'higher for longer' stance as recent CPI prints suggested inflation was stickier than anticipated.

• Wholesale prices often serve as a leading indicator for consumer inflation as costs are passed down the supply chain.

• Markets have been volatile as investors look for 'Goldilocks' data—weak enough to justify cuts but strong enough to avoid recession.
happened:
• The Bureau of Labor Statistics reported a 0.3% monthly rise in PPI, dragging the annual rate down to 3.2% from 3.7% in January.

• US 10-year Treasury yields retreated to approximately 4.53% as bond prices rose on the cooling inflation narrative.

• Gold prices remained resilient near all-time highs, trading above $2,650 per ounce as real yields softened.
implications:
• A sustained downtrend in PPI increases the likelihood of a 'dovish' tilt in upcoming Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) minutes.

• Lower input costs may help preserve corporate profit margins, which have been under pressure from rising wages.

• Persistent weakness in manufacturing data alongside cooling prices could reignite 'hard landing' fears if retail sales also falter.
importance:
• Directly impacts Fed policy expectations, the single largest driver of global asset valuations.

• Affects the US Dollar's strength, which has significant ramifications for emerging market debt and commodities.

• PPI serves as a crucial check on the 'reinflation' narrative that had been building in early 2026.

title: Copper Forecasts Upgraded Amid AI Infrastructure Buildout
content: Analysts have upgraded their 2026 copper price forecasts, citing a 'sustained supply-demand deficit' driven by the rapid expansion of AI data centres and global electrification. Reuters polling now suggests copper could deliver 20% gains over the coming year as structural supply constraints in mining persist.
summary: Copper prices are projected to surge as AI data centres and the energy transition outstrip mining capacity.
context:
• Copper is often called 'Doctor Copper' because its price is seen as a reliable indicator of global economic health.

• AI data centres require significantly more copper for power distribution and cooling systems than traditional server farms.

• Major mining projects in South America have faced regulatory and social hurdles, limiting new supply reaching the market.
happened:
• LME copper futures trended toward $12,000 per ton following analyst reports highlighting a 20% projected price increase for 2026.

• Junior mining stocks saw a surge in volume as investors sought leveraged exposure to the anticipated supply crunch.

• Major producers like BHP and Freeport-McMoRan issued updates noting tight inventories and robust forward orders.
implications:
• Rising copper prices could contribute to 'green inflation,' making the energy transition more expensive for governments.

• Industrial manufacturers may face margin compression if they cannot pass higher raw material costs to consumers.

• Increased M&A activity is expected in the mining sector as majors look to acquire proven reserves to meet future demand.
importance:
• Critical for investors in the commodities and basic materials sectors.

• Impacts the cost structure of the tech industry, specifically firms building out AI infrastructure.

• Serves as a hedge against a broader industrial recovery in 2026.
